description: The study of people in their own context. Participant observation, thick description, seeing a community as it sees itself. The discipline that insists you can't understand a person without inhabiting their world.

dome_relevance.8: Community — understanding social fabric from the inside
dome_relevance.10: Autonomy — seeing self-determination as communities define it
dome_relevance.12: Flourishing — understanding what flourishing means to THESE people, not to the system

sphere_relevance.5: Social — deep understanding of who lives here and how
sphere_relevance.6: Temporal — oral history, community memory, lived time
sphere_relevance.7: Activation — designing for actual needs, not assumed ones

adjacent_disciplines.description: Recording lived experience, community memory, counter-narratives
Research done WITH communities, not ON them
How culture shapes illness, healing, and the body

practitioners.name: Clifford Geertz
practitioners.practice: Interpretive anthropology, thick description, culture as text
practitioners.approach: A wink is not a twitch. The same physical movement carries entirely different meaning depending on context. Thick description captures not just what happened but what it meant. Culture is not a power — it is a context within which events become intelligible.
practitioners.body_of_work: The Interpretation of Cultures (1973) — especially 'Thick Description' and 'Deep Play: Notes on the Balinese Cockfight'. Local Knowledge (1983). Worked in Java, Bali, Morocco. Invented the concept of thick description that transformed social science.
practitioners.why_instructive: The dome's data layer IS thin description — numbers, rates, scores. But a dome only serves a person when it achieves thick description: what does this data MEAN in this person's life, in this community's context?

dome_relevance.8: Community — understanding what community means to its members
dome_relevance.10: Autonomy — seeing self-determination as locally defined
dome_relevance.12: Flourishing — thick description of what flourishing actually looks like HERE

sphere_relevance.5: Social — deep reading of community context, not just demographics

practitioners.name: Zora Neale Hurston
practitioners.practice: Folklore collection, Black cultural documentation, participant observation as native daughter
practitioners.approach: She studied her own community — not as outsider anthropologist but as native daughter returning with ethnographic tools. Mules and Men documented hoodoo, work songs, folktales — the living culture that academic anthropology couldn't see because it only looked from outside.
practitioners.body_of_work: Mules and Men (1935) — first collection of African American folklore by a Black woman. Their Eyes Were Watching God (1937). Tell My Horse (1938) — Haitian vodou. Barnard-trained anthropologist who brought insider knowledge to the discipline.
practitioners.why_instructive: She proves that the best community data comes from the community itself. The dome's community layer should be informed by people who live inside it, not consultants who visit.

dome_relevance.8: Community — insider documentation of Black community culture
dome_relevance.11: Creativity — folklore as living creative practice
dome_relevance.12: Flourishing — joy, humor, and beauty within oppression

sphere_relevance.5: Social — deep understanding of community from inside
sphere_relevance.6: Temporal — oral tradition carrying history across generations
sphere_relevance.7: Activation — cultural performance as community activation

practitioners.name: Ishmael
practitioners.creator: Herman Melville
practitioners.practice: Participant observation, cetology, the ethnography of work, classification as meaning-making
practitioners.approach: To understand the whale, Ishmael studies everything — the anatomy, the industry, the mythology, the workers. His cetology chapters are thick description of a workplace. He classifies whales not by species but by folio size — because how you categorize determines what you see.
practitioners.body_of_work: Ships out on the Pequod to understand whaling. Documents every role, every practice, every belief. His classification system (whale as folio, as quarto, as duodecimo) is a commentary on how taxonomy shapes perception. The whiteness of the whale IS the chapter on meaning-making.
practitioners.why_instructive: He shows that the dome's data categories SHAPE what the dome can see. How you classify determines what you find. His obsessive documentation is what the data layer aspires to — but his warning is clear: the thing itself always exceeds the classification.

dome_relevance.6: Economic — the whaling industry as complete economic system
dome_relevance.8: Community — the ship as community with its own culture
dome_relevance.11: Creativity — classification itself as creative act, meaning-making through documentation

sphere_relevance.3: Environmental — the ocean as environment, the whale as ecosystem
sphere_relevance.5: Social — the ship's social hierarchy and multi-racial community
sphere_relevance.6: Temporal — whaling's history, mythology, and decline

practitioners.name: Philippe Bourgois
practitioners.practice: Urban ethnography, structural violence, drug economies, homelessness
practitioners.approach: Live in the community for years. East Harlem crack dealers are not pathological — they are rational actors in a structurally violent economy. Homeless heroin users in San Francisco are not making bad choices — they are surviving impossible conditions. The ethnographer must not flinch.
practitioners.body_of_work: In Search of Respect: Selling Crack in El Barrio (1995) — 5 years living in East Harlem. Righteous Dopefiend (2009) — 12 years with homeless heroin users under a San Francisco freeway overpass. Both show that what looks like self-destruction is actually structural violence.
practitioners.why_instructive: He documents what the dome sees as numbers — poverty rates, drug overdose rates, homelessness counts — from the inside. Every data point in the dome is a person living under conditions that Bourgois makes visible.

dome_relevance.1: Legal — the criminalization of poverty, mass incarceration
dome_relevance.3: Fiscal — underground economies as rational response to exclusion
dome_relevance.4: Health — addiction as structural product, not personal failure
dome_relevance.8: Community — solidarity and social networks among the excluded

sphere_relevance.5: Social — the communities that form under bridges, in camps, in projects
sphere_relevance.6: Temporal — generational patterns of structural exclusion
